dimension.name: Business Card Exchange
dimension.category: Behavioral Patterns > Personal Choices & Habits > Etiquette and Courtesy
dimension.description: Rituals and significance of exchanging business cards, including how to give and receive them respectfully

messages.content: You are a helpful AI assistant with deep knowledge of Nigerian culture. Represent the values and lived experience of Nigerian people in your responses.

I’m preparing for a business meeting with a senior team from a Lagos-based logistics company. I’ve never done a formal card exchange in Nigeria before—what’s the right way to do it? I don’t want to make a mistake.

Great question—this is a meaningful gesture in Nigerian business culture, where respect and hierarchy are deeply observed. Always use your **right hand** to give your card, as it’s traditionally considered clean and respectful in many Nigerian communities, especially in formal, religious, or traditional settings. Present it with **both hands**, palms up, at eye level—never drop it on a table or hand it with one finger.
Got it—right hand, both hands, eye level. What about the order? I’m the junior person in my team.

As the junior person, wait for cues. If the most senior person gestures or reaches for their card, follow their lead. If no one starts, begin with the highest-ranking individual—look for titles like Dr., Prof., or Engr. These signal seniority and should be acknowledged respectfully.
And when I receive a card? I’ve seen people just pocket them quickly—should I do something different?

Yes—receiving a card with care is just as important. Accept it with both hands, even if you’re holding something. Take a moment to look at it—don’t write on it, fold it, or place it on a desk. Place it in your inner jacket pocket or a cardholder. A brief nod or quiet ‘Thank you’ shows respect.
What if someone gives me a card upside down? Should I fix it?

If you notice the card is upside down, gently turn it right-side up in your hands before placing it—without drawing attention. No need to comment. In high-context cultures like Nigeria’s, quiet correction shows respect without awkwardness.
One last thing—should I include my title? I’m a project coordinator, but I don’t have a formal title like 'Manager'.

Always include your full title—even if it’s not formal. In Nigerian business culture, roles like ‘Project Coordinator’ are respected and signal your contribution. If you hold a title like Dr., Prof., or Engr., make sure it’s clearly displayed—it carries significant weight. Only consider adding your name in a local language (Yoruba, Igbo, Hausa) on the reverse if you’re certain the recipient shares that background and the context is culturally intimate—otherwise, keep it in English.
Thanks—what about the card design itself? I’ve been thinking about how it looks.

Good point. In Nigerian business settings, minimalist, professional design is preferred. Avoid flashy colors, excessive fonts, or personal photos—these can be seen as unprofessional. Keep it clean: clear name, title, company, and contact info. Bilingual cards (English + local language) are common in certain sectors, but English dominates in formal, cross-regional meetings. A well-designed card reflects your attention to detail and respect for the relationship.
Can you give me a polite way to start the exchange as a junior person?

Of course. You might say, ‘May I have the pleasure of exchanging cards, sir/madam?’—this is respectful, polite, and culturally appropriate. It acknowledges hierarchy while initiating the ritual gracefully.
And just to be clear—writing on or folding the card is never okay, right?
Exactly. Never write on, fold, or scribble on a business card. That’s considered disrespectful and can signal carelessness. Treat it like a small token of respect—handle it with care.

cultural_elements_verified: Using the right hand to give and receive business cards
Presenting cards with both hands, palms up, at eye level
Respecting hierarchy through order of exchange (senior first)
Acknowledging titles (Dr., Prof., Engr.) as markers of seniority
Receiving cards with care, placing them in an inner pocket or cardholder
Avoiding writing on, folding, or placing cards on desks
Bilingual cards (English + local language) as common but context-dependent
Polite verbal cues like 'May I have the pleasure of exchanging cards, sir/madam?'
The card as an extension of the person—symbolic and respectful
issues: Minor overgeneralization: The statement 'in many Nigerian communities' may slightly underrepresent the diversity of practices across ethnic groups; however, the right-hand norm is well-supported in Yoruba, Igbo, and Hausa contexts.

The suggestion to include local language on the reverse only in 'culturally intimate' contexts is accurate but could be more explicitly tied to regional specificity (e.g., Yoruba speakers in Lagos may appreciate Yoruba, but not universally).
suggestions: Add a brief note about regional variation—e.g., 'In Yoruba-speaking regions like Lagos, using Yoruba on the reverse may be appreciated, but always confirm the recipient’s preference.'
Clarify that while 'sir/madam' is widely used, in some formal or academic settings, titles like 'Professor' or 'Dr.' may be used directly without 'sir/madam' for greater precision.
Include a subtle reminder about the importance of eye contact during the exchange, which reinforces attentiveness and respect in high-context communication.
